content.full_text:
This letter is in response to your request for advice, on behalf of the San Diego
County Regional Airport Authority (the "RAA"), regarding the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").I Since your request asks foi geneial guidance and has not
identified specific officials on whose behalf you are requesting advice, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.' Please note that the Commission is not
permitted to offer advice on bodies of law outside the confines of the Act. In addition,
the Commission does not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation
18329(bX8)(A), enclosed.) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct, which may have already taken place.
Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACTTcEs C ouurssloN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
June 30, 2006
Amy Gonzalez
Senior Assistant General Counsel
San Diego County
Regional Airport Authority
P.O. Box 82776
San Diego, CA 92138-27 7 6
RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-06-103
Dear Ms. Gonzalez
QUESTION
Would an individual member of the RAA's board, including the board's executive
committee, have a conflict of interest, under the provisions of the Act, if the member
contributes personal funds to a political action committee ("PAC") to support or oppose a
ballot measure that the RAA is charged with submitting in the form of a countywide
election ballot?
' Goremm"nt Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations. All references to sections herein are to the
Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831141' regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-06-103
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
Under the Act, a board member would not have a conflict of interest by virtue of
the board member contributing personal funds to a PAC to support or oppose a ballot
measure, as there is no governmental decision involved. However, a board member,
whose economic interest (as enumerated in the Act) may be financially affected, may
have a disqualifying conflict of interest prohibiting the board member from making,
patticipating in, or influencing any governmental decision relating to the ballot measure.
We are unable to offer you advice on bodies of law thatmay also be relevant to
your situation, but which lie outside the Act and are therefore beyond our jurisdiction.
We refer you instead to the Office of the Attorney General who can provide you with
information regarding other bodies of law, which may be applicable to your question.
FACTS
The RAA is an independent local govemment agency formed pursuant to public
Utilities Code section 172002. Under Public Utilities Code section 170048(h), the RAA
shall adopt a comprehensive plan on the future development of San Diego's Regional
International Airport. Additionally, the RAA shall submit the particular site
recommendation in the form of a local ballot proposition to the San Diego County
Registrar of voters for placement on either the November 2,2004, or the
November 7,2006, countywide election ballot.
A nine-member board govems the RAA. The members of the board are
appointed pursuant to Public Utilities Code section 170016. A three-member executive
committee is included in the nine-member board. While the executive committee
members receive a base salary commensurate to that of a San Diego County Superior
Court judge (Id. section 170028(e)), the remaining members of the board do not receive
such base salary.
In a telephone conversation on June 26,2006, you indicated that you were
concerned with a possible conflict of interest if an executive committee board member
contributes personal funds to a PAC in favor of passing the ballot initiative. You have
indicated that you do not seek advice as to an individual board member's committee
reporting requirements or personal campaign activities. Additionally, you have indicated
that you do not seek advice as to the use of public funds as the board member seeks to
contribute only personal funds.3
3 Whil" you had initially asked whether a contribution from a board member's personal funds to a
PAC would be subject to a contribution limit, this question is only relevant to a board members personal
campaign activities and is not relevant to the conflict-of-interest analysis you requested on June 26,2006.
Should an individual board member have questions related to the board memberi obligations under the Act
when contributing personal funds, it is advisable that the board member seek additionul udri." providing
all relevant facts.


File No. I-06-103
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
To begin, we note that the Commission may only offer advice within the confines
of the Act. Other bodies of law, which lie outside the Act and are therefore beyond our
jurisdiction, may be relevant to your situation such as the common-law conflicl of interest
doctrine. As we cannot offer advice outside the confines of the Act, we refer you instead
to the Office of the Attorney General who can provide you with information regarding
other bodies of law, which may be applicable to your question.
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govenimental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interes t, in a
goverimental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the governmental decision will have a material financial effect on one o, *o." of the
public official's economic interests. (Section 87ll3;regulation 1g700(a).) The
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding wheiher an
individual has a disqualiflring conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
(Regulation 1 8700(b)( 1 )-(8).)
Step One: Is the board member a ,.public official?r,
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,,
(sections 87100, 87103; regulation 18700(bxr).) A "public official,, is ..every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency....,,
(Section 82048.) As members of the RAA board, alocalgovemment agency,the board
members are indeed public officials within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Is the board member making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to disqualify a public official
from "making," "participating in making," or "influencing,, u gori.n nentaj decision.
Making a Governmental Decision: Apublic official "makes a governmental
decision" when the official, acting within the authority of his or her ofice or position,
votes on a matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course
of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or h". ug.n"y.
(Section 87 1 00; regulation 187 02.1 (a).)
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates
in making a governmental decision" when, actingwithin the authority of his oi h",
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises or makes recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental


File No. I-06-103
Page No. 4
decision. (Section 87 I 00; regulation I g7 02.2.)
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules as to whether a public
official uses or attempts to use his or h_er official position to influence a governmental
decision' The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the
public official's own agency, or an age-ncy appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of the public official's agency, bui the public official is not a decision-maker per
se' (Regulation 1s702.3(a)-) In these cases, ifi'the official contacts, o. upp"u., before, or
otherwise attempts to influenc e, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency" then he or she is attempting to influence a govemmental decision. This includes,
but is not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business
entity, client, or customer.,'
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an
agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject
to the budgetary control of the public officialis u["ry. @egulation 1s702.3(b).) Under
this rule, the official cannot act or purport "to act on u"rraro[ or as the representative of
his or her agency to any member, offic"r, emproyee or consultant of an agency,, to
influence a decision that will have a r"urorubly ibreseeable material financial effect on
his or her economic interests.
According to your facts, a board member is considering contributing personal
funds to a PAC to support or oppose a ballot measure. This personal decision does not
involve a governmental action- Since this decision is not a govefirmental decision, the
conflict-of-interest rules are not implicated_and the analysis ends. In short, under your
facts, the board member would not have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the
Act's provisions by virtue of his or her pe.sonul contrii'utions.
You have also stated that the RAA is required to submit the ballot measure to the
register of voters for inclusion on the ballot. Therefore, a board member, whose
economic interest (as enumerated in section 87103) may be financially affected, may
indeed have a disqualifying conflict of interest as to any governmental decisionsrelated
to the measure. In other words, the conclusion that u Uou?a member does not have a
disqualifying conflict of interest preventing him from making a contribution to a pAC
does not preclude the possibility thatthe member mayhave a disqualifying "orfli"t of
interest in any governmental decisions related to the Lailot,such as placing the measure
on the ballot, determining the content of the ballot, or impiementing the measure if
enacted.a
a To assist in determining whether a board member may have a disqualifiing conflict of interest
in any particular governmental decision, we have enclosed the commission fact sheet entitled ,,,canl
vote? overview of conflicts Laws," which details the full "igh;-;;;;ralysis of conflict of interest
questions.


File No. I-06-103
Page No. 5
If you have other questions on this matter,prease contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
BGL:rd
